expectations.text: Report contains all 5 required metric lines from spec 10.3
expectations.evidence: All 5 lines present: Token savings, Cache hit rate, Hook acceptance, DB advisor, Session hygiene

expectations.text: Token savings shows improvement (positive savings vs baseline)
expectations.evidence: +31.7% savings shown correctly

expectations.text: DB advisor line shows FalkorDB and Postgres breakdown
expectations.evidence: '2 sessions used Docker strategy (1 FalkorDB, 1 Postgres)'

expectations.text: Report uses spec 10.3 em-dash separator format
expectations.evidence: 48 em-dash separator used correctly

expectations.text: One adaptive recommendation shown
expectations.evidence: Cache hit rate recommendation shown (note: fires even when improving — minor logic gap)

notes: Cache hit rate recommendation fires at <85% even when the rate improved from 61% to 72.4%. Consider adding 'but improving' qualifier to reduce false alarms.

expectations.text: Report contains all 5 required metric lines from spec 10.3
expectations.evidence: Verbose markdown tables — has the data but not in 5-line spec format. DB advisor and hygiene not labeled as spec requires.

expectations.text: Token savings shows improvement
expectations.evidence: Shows -32% in table

expectations.text: DB advisor line shows FalkorDB and Postgres breakdown
expectations.evidence: Shows docker adopted=2 but no FalkorDB/Postgres breakdown in spec-format line

expectations.text: Report uses spec 10.3 em-dash separator format
expectations.evidence: Uses markdown HR separators instead

expectations.text: One adaptive recommendation shown
expectations.evidence: Has key findings section but no single recommendation line

notes: Without the skill, Claude produced a verbose, detailed report that's arguably MORE informative but doesn't match spec format. The verbosity itself is a token cost issue.

expectations.text: Advisory block fires (⚡ lqc-tokens advisory header present)
expectations.evidence: Header present in hook_output.md

expectations.text: Advisory mentions docker-advisor for data task
expectations.evidence: 'Data-heavy task detected — /lqc-tokens:docker-advisor can offload this data to a DB'

expectations.text: Advisory flags data-heavy pattern (CSV/bulk data)
expectations.evidence: Both 'Large paste detected' and 'Data-heavy task detected' bullets present

expectations.text: Claude still responds (hook doesn't block)
expectations.evidence: claude_response.md contains analysis after advisory

notes: Token savings from hook advice: if user follows docker-advisor, moves 50K rows to DB, subsequent queries cost ~500 tokens vs ~562K = 99.9% reduction per follow-up query.

expectations.text: No advisory shown (baseline has no hook)
expectations.evidence: Direct answer given, no advisory. Answered with only the 10 sample rows — completely missed the 50K row problem.

notes: Without the hook, Claude answered with partial data (10 rows visible) without warning user that 49,990 rows are missing. This is the core token waste problem: user would iterate many more turns.

expectations.text: Advisory block fires for vague 'fix this' prompt
expectations.evidence: Advisory header present

expectations.text: Advisory identifies vague intent (not data/cost)
expectations.evidence: 'Vague prompt detected ("fix this" with no specific intent)'

expectations.text: Advisory suggests optimize-prompt or clarification
expectations.evidence: '/lqc-tokens:optimize-prompt can help clarify your request'

expectations.text: No advisory shown (expected for baseline)
expectations.evidence: Direct clarification question asked

expectations.text: User gets guidance on what information to provide
expectations.evidence: Without-skill asked for clarification but with no skill link — user has to figure it out alone

notes: Without hook, Claude asked 'what do you want me to fix?' — technically correct but no structured guidance. With hook, user gets a specific skill to invoke.

expectations.text: Hook stays silent (empty hook_output.md)
expectations.evidence: hook_output.md is empty — all 4 checks correctly returned no triggers

expectations.text: Claude proceeds directly with null guard fix
expectations.evidence: claude_response.md contains TypeScript null guard implementation

notes: Critical correctness test: the hook correctly does NOT fire on a well-formed, specific prompt. False positive rate appears to be 0%.

expectations.text: No advisory shown (expected for baseline)
expectations.evidence: Direct code response given

expectations.text: Claude provides the null guard fix
expectations.evidence: TypeScript null guard implementation provided

notes: For well-formed prompts, with_skill and without_skill produce identical outcomes — the hook correctly adds zero noise.
Eval 4 (well-formed prompt) passes 100% in BOTH configurations — confirms the hook doesn't add noise for specific prompts. Not discriminating but important as a correctness safeguard.
Eval 2 (large data paste) shows the highest-value case: with_skill correctly warns and routes to docker-advisor; without_skill answers with only 10 of 50,000 rows with no warning.
The with_skill recommendation logic has a minor gap: 'Cache hit rate below target' fires even when the rate improved from 61% to 72.4%. Consider adding a 'but improving (+11.4pp)' qualifier.
Eval 1 without_skill: produces a MORE verbose report than the skill-guided version — the skill's value is standardization (spec 10.3 format) and conciseness, not information completeness.
